ai_strategy.summary: CU Boulder has public AI guidance through OIT, data-security guidelines, communications guidance, teaching-and-learning discussions, and strong AI research activity in computer science and environmental data science. The posture is cautious and risk-aware, emphasizing approved tools, public-data-only use, syllabus clarity, sustainability, privacy, and security.

key_provisions: Use AI in accordance with academic integrity expectations and instructor or unit guidance
Do not enter restricted, confidential, regulated, or sensitive data into unapproved AI tools
Review AI outputs for accuracy, hallucination, bias, copyright, and attribution issues
Disclose or cite AI use when required by course, research, publisher, or unit rules
Keep human judgment and accountability in consequential academic, research, and administrative decisions
ai_policy.summary: University of Colorado Boulder's AI policy posture is best characterized as cautious: public sources show explicit expectations for responsible use, privacy, disclosure, academic integrity, and human review, while leaving some implementation decisions to units or instructors.

bodies.name: Office of Information Technology
bodies.scope: AI tools, resources, data security, and campus guidance.

bodies.name: Strategic Relations and Communications
bodies.scope: Guiding principles for generative AI in communications.

governance_structure.summary: University of Colorado Boulder's public AI governance is organized through Office of Information Technology, Strategic Relations and Communications.

initiatives.title: Systemwide ChatGPT Edu agreement
initiatives.description: The University of Colorado entered a three-year agreement with OpenAI to provide ChatGPT Edu across the system, with each campus standing up its own secure environment for eligible students, faculty, and staff. The CU system FAQ says the current contract includes $2 million in annual licensing costs supporting 100,000 users.
initiatives.significance: This closes a common public-data gap by disclosing both broad AI platform scope and annual licensing scale for a major public university system.

investment.description: The CU system states the first-year ChatGPT Edu contract is paid from the System Office and includes $2 million in annual licensing costs supporting 100,000 users.

collaborations.description: Three-year renewable agreement for systemwide ChatGPT Edu environments.

tools_and_vendors.name: ChatGPT Edu
tools_and_vendors.role: Secure generative AI environment for eligible CU students, faculty, and staff

initiatives.policy_implications: CU requires training, says ordinary university policies still apply, and says OpenAI will not use CU environments to train models.

computing_infrastructure.summary: CU Boulder's Alpine hardware documentation lists GPU-enabled partitions with 21 AMD MI100 GPUs, 33 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, 9 NVIDIA L40 GPUs, and 2 NVIDIA Grace-Hopper GPUs, for 65 listed GPUs across the regular GPU partitions. Alpine is jointly funded by CU Boulder, CU Anschutz, Colorado State University, and NSF.

investments_and_gifts.title: ChatGPT Edu annual licensing
investments_and_gifts.purpose: Systemwide ChatGPT Edu licensing for eligible students, faculty, and staff across CU campuses.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: AI and sustainability guidance
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: CU Boulder guidance explicitly asks users to consider environmental impacts of generative AI and to use lower-impact alternatives when appropriate.

regulatory_context.summary: University of Colorado Boulder operates in Colorado and within University of Colorado; public AI guidance should be read alongside applicable state, federal, and system privacy, security, accessibility, and academic-integrity requirements.

narrative_summary: University of Colorado Boulder (Boulder, CO) is an R1 institution whose public AI posture shows activity across governance, teaching, research, enterprise tooling, workforce readiness, and access. CU Boulder has public AI guidance through OIT, data-security guidelines, communications guidance, teaching-and-learning discussions, and strong AI research activity in computer science and environmental data science. The posture is cautious and risk-aware, emphasizing approved tools, public-data-only use, syllabus clarity, sustainability, privacy, and security. The most concrete public initiatives captured here are AI Guidance at CU Boulder; AI Data Security Guidelines; Campus LLM Gateway initiative; CU Boulder AI research; Generative AI guiding principles for communicators. Across the profile, the strongest evidence comes from official university AI hubs, policy pages, teaching resources, research-center pages, and program pages rather than secondary reporting. The institution_type is set to r1_research from the canonical Carnegie R1 list. Known gaps are intentionally explicit: leadership names, user counts, detailed budgets, and compute counts are only included when they surfaced in public sources. Overall, the profile supports at least five AI initiatives and evidences strategy/leadership, governance, teaching/curriculum, research infrastructure, student services or enterprise tooling, workforce/community, partnerships, and equity dimensions where public sources permit.

gaps_acknowledged: No central chief AI officer surfaced in reviewed public sources.
ChatGPT Edu annual licensing cost and user capacity are public at the CU system level, but CU Boulder-specific active-user adoption metrics remain undisclosed.
Alpine GPU partition counts are public; no separate AI-only GPU allocation or utilization metric was found.
Enrollment was left null rather than relying on non-source local data.
